turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in Case 14짯10154, Voisine and Armstrong v. United States. Ms. Villa. APPOINTED BY THIS COURT

turns.name: VIRGINIA G. VILLA
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: There are three reasons to exclude reckless misdemeanors from the phrase "use... of physical force" as occurs in 921(a)(33). The first is that it's consistent with the definition of use that this Court has implemented in other cases. Second, it is consistent with the common짯law definition of battery. And, third, it is consistent with the intentionality inherent in domestic violence.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But it's inconsistent with the treatment of bodily injury. I mean, I think you agree, because the Court so held, that either bodily injury or offensive touching is the act 짯짯 satisfies the act requirement. And you say if there's bodily injury, then reckless is enough, but if it's only offensive touching, then you need a higher mental state, intentional or knowing? Why 짯짯 if 짯짯 if both acts qualify equally, why shouldn't the same state of mind attach to each?

turns.name: VIRGINIA G. VILLA
turns.text: The same state of mind should attach to each, and that is why intentionality is the state of mind that under the common law can attach to each. Because intentional battery covered both the offensive physical contact as well as the physical injury, whereas recklessness covers only bodily injury.

turns.text: I'm sorry. Intentionality, in my mind, is misperceived, because you think 짯짯 you're talking about intentionality as the act of causing the injury. It wasn't 짯짯 it wasn't to cause the injury, but the act caused the injury.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR

turns.name: VIRGINIA G. VILLA

That's what recklessness is. And so that's the difference. I would like to reserve the rest of my time for rebuttal, please.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Ms. Eisenstein.

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This Court has twice interpreted Section 922(g)(9) in Hazen and Castleman, and each time concluded that Congress intended to keep guns away from those convicted of assault and battery under generally applicable State and Federal and Tribal law. Petitioner attempts to distinguish between offenses that cause bodily injury and those that involve offensive touching. But as this Court already decided in Castleman, that is a fallacy; that distinction is elusive. And that's because States, including those that prohibit the causation of bodily injury, even those offenses may include offenses that are similar in terms of degree to offensive touching. In fact, that was what was at issue at Castleman itself. It was the bodily injury prong of the Tennessee statute, not the offensive touching prong. And 짯짯 and in that case, the bodily injury could be committed in a way that involved only slight injury. And yet, this Court refused to distinguish, by degree of force, batteries that qualify Section 922(g)(9) predicates. Keep in mind that States overwhelmingly define battery as 짯짯 only as to the resulting injury. Petitioners' role, which would limit misdemeanor crimes of domestic violence to intentional conduct, would require a prosecutor in those jurisdictions to prove in the underlying offense not only that the contact and the push, the hit, the grab was intentional, but that prosecutor would also have to show that the abuser intended the resulting injury in order to prove the battery offense under the vast majority of State and under Federal law.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: What's so bad about that? I mean, given her door example, for example, why should the accidental or perhaps you would say she 짯짯 you know, he or she knew that the person was close, why should that constitute misdemeanor crime of domestic violence?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Well, in part because Congress intended to cover batteries. And the way 짯짯 not only at common law, but also in modern 짯짯 under the common and generic understanding of battery, define a battery offense as 짯짯 only as to the causation of the resulting harm. And keep in mind, reckless conduct 짯짯 and petitioner avoids the fact of what reckless conduct is. A reckless conduct is not an involuntary or a negligent, or even an accidental act. Reckless conduct is voluntary conduct. It's conduct where an individual chooses to act in conscious disregard of a substantial and known risk of harm. So if the 짯짯 if the person in your example, Mr. Chief Justice, slammed the door not realizing the fingers were there, that's not going to be reckless conduct at all. That would be negligence at best.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, I mean, you don't know; you're running away. What if it's a close call? You don't pause to think, well, gosh, maybe he's close enough or she's close enough that this will, you know, hurt his fingers or something. I mean, is 짯짯 is that recklessness?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: I think to the degree to which the 짯짯 the individual consciously disregards the substantial 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Yeah. You don't want to turn around and see how close the person chasing you is, if you slam the door, oh, gosh, that's going to pinch the fingers or something.

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: But 짯짯 but I think that the 짯짯 what feels wrong about the hypothetical is not the 짯짯 the degree of intent of the act or 짯짯 it's the extent to which that person may be justified in the door slamming, which was that this was an act, perhaps, of self짯defense. I think the more prototypical examples are situations where 짯짯 that this Court highlighted in Castleman, where an individual takes intentional action to make contact with their loved one and does so in a way that is reckless as to whether, for example, if there's a push, reckless as to whether his wife simply stumbles backwards or falls down and injures herself. And that's the way States overwhelmingly frame their assault and battery statutes. And that's what 짯짯

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I think that under an assault, a general assault and battery statute, it's theoretically possible that a person could be convicted of battering his or her spouse under those circumstances. I don't think that's problematic for two reasons. One is I think it's an extremely narrow category of offenses, and Petitioner has pointed to no actual examples.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, the 짯짯 the main statute says "recklessly causes bodily injury."

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: So there 짯짯 and I agree with Your Honor that in theory, that type of offense could constitute a battery. But to the degree to which that's prosecuted where 짯짯 in the real world that's prosecuted where the 짯짯 a domestic family member is the sole victim and that that is only a misdemeanor, that there's not sufficiently severe injury or other factors that rise to the level of a felony offense, which would be disqualifying under its own right, I don't think that that level of overbreadth is problematic under the statute.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I'm not talking about overbreadth. I'm talking about interpretation of the statute. The statute includes a misdemeanor, so that disposes of that part of your answer. The statute covers a misdemeanor.

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Correct, Your Honor. But what I'm 짯짯 but my point is that the type of scenario that 짯짯 that is posited where both there would be a reckless, sort of a generally reckless conduct, that the victim happens to be, by 짯짯 by chance the family member, as opposed to another member of the public, and thus qualifying as a Section 922(g)(9) predicate is narrow indeed. And the converse of that is striking. While the 짯짯 while the overbreadth of the hypothetical 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: It's narrow, but it seems to qualify under the statute. I don't quite understand your answer. You said, oh, well, it's narrow. But it's still a violation under the meaning of the statute.

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: I think it's a violation under the meaning of general assault and battery, but I also think that there's nothing wrong with 짯짯 as Justice Kagan suggested, with treating that type of offense as disqualifying.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: So you're saying that the example is covered by the statute.

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. And I think, though, that to the extent to which that raises a concern in terms of overbreadth, I don't think this Court should be concerned about that, because what Congress was worried about wasn't an overbreadth of these prosecutions against family members. Instead, they went to a real underreporting, under prosecution and the low conviction rates of these offenses. This was the problem that Congress addressed. And the whole reason why they extended the firearms ban from felony offenses 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Which of our 짯짯 which of our cases say that you don't have to worry if the categorical approach covers conduct of the sort Justice Kennedy was talking about because Congress meant to cover other things?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I think that you're right that the categorical approach and its strictures can cause real problems if there's any degree of overbreadth in the statute. But that's exactly why, in interpreting Section 922(g)(9) and what Congress's purpose was is extremely important here. Because to the extent to which Petitioner's view is adopted, which would require only intentional conduct to qualify, any degree of overbreadth such as Justice Kennedy's one example in a State, of California of a driving offense would exclude the entire statute, because 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I'm not sure "overbreadth" 짯짯 I'm not sure overbreadth is the right legal term. So you think it's an appropriate defense under a prosecution here that the type of conduct I was charged with is really not what Congress had in mind? Yes, it fits under the terms of the statute, but it's not what Congress had in mind. And there's going to be argument about that.

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: No, Your Honor. The 짯짯 my argument is the opposite, which is that this Court should effectuate Congress's purpose by giving Section 922(g)(9) the meaning that Congress intended, which is to cover generally applicable assault and battery statutes regardless of whether, on a rare instance, they may end up covering an individual who was recklessly driving and injured a family member.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: I feel like your answer was that the statute does cover it, but it's most unlikely that a prosecutor would bring such a case. I think that's what you started to say about it.

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. I think it's unlikely that those prosecutions would be brought in the State offense on those 짯짯 under those circumstances and that there's no reason to drastically constrain the interpretation of Section 922(g)(9).

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Do we have precedent from this Court saying it's okay, we can trust the prosecutors to do the right thing? Can you cite me a case that says that?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Your Honor, I 짯짯 I 짯짯 I do not 짯짯 I do not represent that. But what I do suggest is what we're looking at here is not what prosecutors might do under the 짯짯 in the State 짯짯 the underlying State prosecution, but what Congress intended to cover under Section 922(g)(9). So in order to avoid a hypothetical scenario where a family member may be injured in a nondomestic context, the result of requiring intentionality as to both the harm and the 짯짯 the touching would be to eliminate all of the statutes that Congress, in fact, intended to cover.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I don't know how 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: What about the 짯짯 what about the 짯짯 the rule of lenity? We are reviewing a decision of a court that divided, and both judges wrote very strong opinions. And we also have 18 U.S.C. Section 16, where a crime of violence doesn't include reckless 짯짯 a reckless state of mind. So putting those together, the other statute, plus that this was a split decision, why doesn't the rule of lenity apply?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Your Honor, the rule of lenity only applies where there is grievous ambiguity in the statute. And I respectfully submit that there is no such grievous ambiguity. Congress's intent was clear, and this Court already found as much, in Castleman and in Hayes, which is that there was a class of offenses that Congress intended to cover by the statute which were generally applicable assault and battery statutes. And the rule that Petitioner suggests would exclude all of those offenses and would frustrate that clear purpose of Congress, so lenity doesn't apply for that reason. And there's no ambiguity, not only as to the purpose, but as to 짯짯 as to what battery itself encompasses. Petitioners have tried to argue that battery, either at common law or in contemporary practice, that there was some requirement of an intent to do harm. And the overwhelming weight and universal weight of authority points the opposite direction. Even the sources cited by Petitioner establish that at common law, there was no requirement for the intent to do harm. And certainly States, in adopting their assault and battery provisions, have modeled that 짯짯 that formulation. Another way to look at this is 짯짯 outside of the common law, is the way that Taylor examined the statute of burglary under the ACCA offense, which is to 짯짯 rather than turning just to the common law, as to view this as covering generic battery. And 짯짯 and when looked at that way under the contemporary practice, the Model Penal Code and the uniform opinion of the courts of appeals and commentators all have concluded that battery was not limited to a purpose to cause a resulting harm, but included contact that could be reckless. In the face of Petitioner's hypotheticals, Petitioner struggles to even conceive of a hypothetical example of where there is a reckless battery that would 짯짯 against a family member that would not constitute a misdemeanor crime of domestic violence. The converse is that Congress enacted Section 19 짯짯 922(g)(9) to address a vital 짯짯 vital public safety problem. It identified those who had been convicted of battering their family members as posing a dramatically increased risk of perpetrating future gun violence against their family. This Court should continue to interpret Section 922(g)(9) in light of that compelling purpose. If there are no further questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Ms. Eisenstein, one question. Can you give me 짯짯 this is a misdemeanor violation. It suspends a constitutional right. Can you give me another area where a misdemeanor violation suspends a constitutional right?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Your Honor, I 짯짯 I'm thinking about that, but I think that the 짯짯 the question is not 짯짯 as I understand Your Honor's question, the culpability necessarily of the act or in terms of the offense 짯짯

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Well, I'm 짯짯 I'm looking at the 짯짯 you're saying that recklessness is sufficient to trigger a violation 짯짯 misdemeanor violation of domestic conduct that results in a lifetime ban on possession of a gun, which, at least as of now, is still a constitutional right.

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Your Honor, to address 짯짯

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Can you think of another constitutional right that can be suspended based upon a misdemeanor violation of a State law?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Your Honor, while I can't think of specifically triggered by a misdemeanor violation, other examples, for example, in the First Amendment context, have allowed for suspension or limitation of a right to free speech or even free association in contexts where there is a compelling interest and risks associated in some cases less than a compelling interest under intermediate scrutiny.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: I'm 짯짯 this is a 짯짯 how long is this suspension of the right to own a firearm?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Your Honor, the right is suspended indefinitely.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Okay. So can you think of a First Amendment suspension or a suspension of a First Amendment right that is permanent?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Your Honor, it's not necessarily permanent as to the individual, but it may be permanent as to a particular harm. And here Congress decided to intervene at the first instance that an individual is convicted of battering their family members because it 짯짯 it relied on substantial and well짯documented evidence that those individuals pose a 짯짯 a long짯term and substantial 짯짯

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So that the 짯짯 again, the suspension is not directly related to the use of the weapon. It is a suspension that is actually indirectly related or actually unrelated. It's just a family member's involved in a misdemeanor violation; therefore, a constitutional right is suspended.

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, but I believe that in terms of the 짯짯 the relationship between Congress's decision to try to prevent domestic gun violence and its means of doing so 짯짯

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I think the studies that Congress relied upon in formulating the 짯짯 the misdemeanor crime of domestic violence ban didn't 짯짯 were directly about the use of a gun because what they showed is that individuals who have previously been 짯짯 battered their spouses, pose up to a six짯fold greater risk of killing, by a gun, their family member.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Well, let's 짯짯 let's say that a publisher is reckless about the use of children, and what could be considered indecent displays and that that triggers a violation of, say, a hypothetical law against the use of children in these ads, and let's say it's a misdemeanor violation. Could you suspend that publisher's right to ever publish again?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Your Honor, I don't think you could suspend the right to ever publish again, but I think that you could limit, for example, the manner and means by which publisher 짯짯

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So how is that different from suspending your Second Amendment right?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN
turns.text: Your Honor, I think that in terms of a 짯짯 the compelling purpose that was identified here, which was the prevention of gun violence and the individual nature of the 짯짯 of the underlying offense, so here this isn't a misdemeanor crime directed at any person at large. These are misdemeanor batteries directed at members 짯짯 specified members of the 짯짯 of that individual's family. Congress 짯짯

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Would you have a better case if this were a gun crime?

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN

That evidence is extremely strong. And Congress recognized that this was a recurring escalating offense. Petitioners are good examples of this. While they didn't reach, thankfully, the point where they were able to reach for a firearm and were prohibited from having a firearm under Federal law, they have each been convicted multiple times of domestic violence offenses and possess the firearms in close proximity. So these aren't individuals who had long짯ago convictions and are suffering from that ban. Congress also contemplated exactly the lifetime nature of the ban that Your Honor suggested and left it in States' hands to resolve that by allowing States to expunge or pardon convictions in cases where an individual either petitions to do so or in some States as a matter of course. So 짯짯 so I understand Your Honor's concern that 짯짯 that this is a potential infringement of individual's Second Amendment rights, but I believe that Congress has identified a compelling purpose and has found a reasonable means of achieving that purpose.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I 짯짯 I suppose one answer is 짯짯 just a partial answer to Justice Thomas's question is SORNA, a violation of sexual offenders have to register before they can travel in interstate commerce. But that's not a prevention from traveling at all. It's just a 짯짯 it's a restriction.

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Do it 짯짯 what is it we have 짯짯 they raised this in their brief. They say, let's focus on the cases in which there is a misdemeanor battery conducted without an intentional or knowing state of mind. turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER

turns.name: ILANA H. EISENSTEIN

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS

turns.name: VIRGINIA G. VILLA
turns.text: Your Honor, Congress did not use the word "battery" in 921(a)(33). And so the question is, is whether, under the rule of lenity, the Court can then say battery is the standard that we are going to use in order to construe this. And it's a push me, pull you because it is the use of force, but that doesn't have to be a prior battery conviction. It could be any misdemeanor conviction that has a state of recklessness for the state of mind and results in any 짯짯 any involvement of a domestic partner. And I think that there is enough play within the statute 짯짯 statute itself, as well as the extent of the common law, so 짯짯 to say that that is a reason for applying the rule of lenity. There is also the underlying constitutional questions. And this Court, as in being able to abrogate a constitutional right indefinitely based on reckless conduct, I believe also presents extreme due process concerns. And for those reasons, I believe that the Court should conclude that under these circumstances, it is best to avoid that question and to construe the statute the way that is consistent with the words. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
